author: @RWBHere
text: Who would have thought that you could do that with a strip of inked paper? Now paint both sides to halve the resistance, and you'll have a better electromagnet. Thanks Robert.

author: @AaronHarper
text:
Great job mate!  The mic is a great addition, and makes your voice so much clearer.


While the motor is cool, it may be of limited power and use since the force is determined by current, limited by both the ink and substrate, flammable paper.  Try instead to build an air core transformer by winding two paper coils around a common core, insulated by a bit of tape.  My guess is that it will be quite effective in the low RF range, which means that the tuning filter of a radio is now possible, since we already know capacitors are feasible.

author: @Northern_Nerd
text:
Oh my, I just read a comment and wondered, could you soak a sewing thread in carbon ink, wrap it around a core and use that for an electromagnet?

Would it work as well as wire? How about as a generator?

replies.author: @jaredhouston4223
replies.text: this is a good idea

replies.author: @craigmccarthy9484
replies.text: Ye I thought it might be a good idea because cotton lasts for years .would have to put a coating on after ink to insulate it

replies.author: @chrisbingham3289
replies.text: As regards to using cotton good idea, as you said it would have to be insulated this could easily be done with a flexible wood varnish. Can't wait to try it have to get the ink first. The trouble I have is I have so many ideas I don't know were to start.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: Yes it would work, but as I see it, insulating it is another challenge. A graphene painted  paper strip is as wide as you need it to be to carry current or lower the net resistance.  And is painted on only one side so the coil wont short itself out.

author: @Northern_Nerd
text:
Dear Robert. 

Very interesting experiment. I had the thought that you could make the solenoid more efficient by using a long strip of paper and a diagonal ink line painted on it so that as it wraps around the core, the ink line wraps the same way a wire would wind around a bobbin. This done correctly might result in a Lighter spool of paper that also mimics a spool of wire.

What do you think?

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: The additional width of a conducting strip lowers the net resistance for a given length of conductor.  You can think of doubling the conductivity width  as having two resistors in parallel instead of just one, and the net resistance of the two being 1/(1/r1 + 1/r2) , with r1=r2 the net resistance then is r1/2, or halfed.

replies.author: @undernetjack
replies.text: You have to insulate the cotton string after. Carefully fully coat it in varnish, or a liquid plasti-dip insulator.  Interesting concept, but very time consuming. Maybe use hemp string , a thicker coating/ insulation and market it as environmentally friendly garden electrical wire? Or if you dont plan to wrap it, a straight piece could in place of copper in low current applications.

author: @cheerdiver
text:
Create a moving solenoid, using the needle.
Thread a small hole in an insulator, 'paint' the thread crest.
Might help to cut threads w/ an insulative type 'greese', ie non-dielectric.

author: @Alexander_Sannikov
text: i mean, sure, you can make a bad solenoid out of anything remotely conductive. you can probably even make a really bad motor. question is, why? graphene ink has interesting surface properties, isn't it more interesting to utilize those instead?

replies.author: @menrodbaquiran2467
replies.text: Well sure but it shows the property of the ink. And why not try all crazy ideas you don't know if you'll find something interesting and new.

replies.author: @dyukk8844
replies.text: Yes like you said Alexander, you can make solenoid from anything "conductive". The tests of painting transformers, solenoid, etc with conductive inks are not new, and they have been around for a some time. But anyway I like RMS work especially the electrolplating projects.
